findings.dominantFinding: The dominant imaging finding is bilateral, solid mass lesions situated within the cerebellopontine angle cisterns, demonstrating characteristic extension into the internal auditory canals. On MRI, these masses are typically isointense to hypointense on T1-weighted imaging, exhibit slightly heterogeneous intermediate-to-high signal on T2-weighted imaging, and show avid, uniform contrast enhancement.
findings.supportingFeatures: Supporting features include expansion and flaring of the internal auditory canals, classically described as having a 'trumpet' or 'ice cream cone' appearance. There may be associated local mass effect causing significant compression of the adjacent brainstem and middle cerebellar peduncles, with potential effacement of the fourth ventricle.
findings.criticalNegatives: Importantly, there is no evidence of a broad dural base with an enhancing dural tail or an obtuse angle with the petrous temporal bone to suggest bilateral meningiomas. Furthermore, the lesions do not follow cerebrospinal fluid signal characteristics on all sequences, ruling out arachnoid or epidermoid cysts.
findings.influentialFeature: The feature that most influences my thinking is the presence of remarkably symmetric, bilateral enhancing masses expanding the internal auditory canals, which is pathognomonic for Neurofibromatosis type 2.

differentials.primaryDiagnosis: The appearances are most in keeping with Bilateral vestibular schwannomas (NF2), given the characteristic solid, avidly enhancing masses expanding both internal auditory canals. Bilateral vestibular schwannomas are the hallmark diagnostic criterion for Neurofibromatosis type 2 (NF2), an autosomal dominant tumour predisposition syndrome.
differentials.principleDifferential: The principle differential would be Vestibular schwannoma, however in a sporadic, non-syndromic setting these present as isolated unilateral lesions rather than the strikingly symmetric bilateral masses seen here. A solitary presentation would prompt consideration of a typical sporadic schwannoma, whereas a bilateral presentation practically guarantees an underlying NF2 genetic mutation.
differentials.excludeDiagnosis: A diagnosis I would want to exclude is Meningioma, given it is the second most common cerebellopontine angle tumour and missing it could alter the surgical approach as it arises from the dura rather than the nerve sheath. However, meningiomas classically form an obtuse angle with the petrous bone, frequently exhibit a dural tail, and do not characteristically widen the internal auditory canal.
differentials.unifyingSummary: Taken together, these findings point to a unifying diagnosis of Neurofibromatosis type 2 (MISME syndrome - Multiple Inherited Schwannomas, Meningiomas, and Ependymomas).

management.priority: From a radiological perspective, the priority is to accurately assess the degree of brainstem compression, verify the patency of the fourth ventricle to rule out obstructive hydrocephalus, and evaluate the entire neuroaxis for synchronous tumours.
management.priorImaging: I would review any available prior imaging to assess disease behaviour — whether this is stable, progressive, or improving. Comparison for interval change is critical to determine the growth rate of these tumours, which guides the timing for surgical intervention or targeted radiotherapy, and to monitor for the emergence of other NF2-associated lesions over time.
management.furtherImaging: I would recommend a contrast-enhanced MRI of the entire brain and whole spine, as this modality is superior here for exquisitely defining brainstem compression and identifying the myriad of other NF2-related lesions, such as multiple meningiomas, spinal ependymomas, or further cranial nerve schwannomas.
management.mriSequences: I would specify high-resolution, heavily T2-weighted 3D sequences (such as CISS or FIESTA) to perfectly delineate the cranial nerves, alongside pre- and post-contrast T1-weighted sequences to thoroughly screen the neuroaxis for associated meningiomas and intramedullary tumours.
management.followUp: If managed conservatively or post-treatment, I would recommend lifelong surveillance MRI follow-up at regular intervals, guided by neuro-oncology protocols, due to the high risk of progressive or new syndromic tumours.
management.mdtDiscussion: This should go to the Neuro-oncology and Skull Base MDT. My report would clearly document the exact dimensions of both masses, the severity of brainstem and fourth ventricular compression, and the presence of any other intracranial or spinal lesions to guide complex multi-staged neurosurgical or radiosurgical management.

vivaAnswer.fullScript:
The dominant imaging finding is bilateral, solid mass lesions situated within the cerebellopontine angle cisterns, demonstrating characteristic extension into the internal auditory canals. On MRI, these masses are typically isointense to hypointense on T1-weighted imaging, exhibit slightly heterogeneous intermediate-to-high signal on T2-weighted imaging, and show avid, uniform contrast enhancement. Supporting features include expansion and flaring of the internal auditory canals, classically described as having a 'trumpet' or 'ice cream cone' appearance. There may be associated local mass effect causing significant compression of the adjacent brainstem and middle cerebellar peduncles, with potential effacement of the fourth ventricle. Importantly, there is no evidence of a broad dural base with an enhancing dural tail or an obtuse angle with the petrous temporal bone to suggest bilateral meningiomas. Furthermore, the lesions do not follow cerebrospinal fluid signal characteristics on all sequences, ruling out arachnoid or epidermoid cysts. The feature that most influences my thinking is the presence of remarkably symmetric, bilateral enhancing masses expanding the internal auditory canals, which is pathognomonic for Neurofibromatosis type 2.

The appearances are most in keeping with Bilateral vestibular schwannomas (NF2), given the characteristic solid, avidly enhancing masses expanding both internal auditory canals. Bilateral vestibular schwannomas are the hallmark diagnostic criterion for Neurofibromatosis type 2 (NF2), an autosomal dominant tumour predisposition syndrome. The principle differential would be Vestibular schwannoma, however in a sporadic, non-syndromic setting these present as isolated unilateral lesions rather than the strikingly symmetric bilateral masses seen here. A solitary presentation would prompt consideration of a typical sporadic schwannoma, whereas a bilateral presentation practically guarantees an underlying NF2 genetic mutation. A diagnosis I would want to exclude is Meningioma, given it is the second most common cerebellopontine angle tumour and missing it could alter the surgical approach as it arises from the dura rather than the nerve sheath. However, meningiomas classically form an obtuse angle with the petrous bone, frequently exhibit a dural tail, and do not characteristically widen the internal auditory canal. Taken together, these findings point to a unifying diagnosis of Neurofibromatosis type 2 (MISME syndrome - Multiple Inherited Schwannomas, Meningiomas, and Ependymomas).

From a radiological perspective, the priority is to accurately assess the degree of brainstem compression, verify the patency of the fourth ventricle to rule out obstructive hydrocephalus, and evaluate the entire neuroaxis for synchronous tumours. I would review any available prior imaging to assess disease behaviour — whether this is stable, progressive, or improving. Comparison for interval change is critical to determine the growth rate of these tumours, which guides the timing for surgical intervention or targeted radiotherapy, and to monitor for the emergence of other NF2-associated lesions over time. I would recommend a contrast-enhanced MRI of the entire brain and whole spine, as this modality is superior here for exquisitely defining brainstem compression and identifying the myriad of other NF2-related lesions, such as multiple meningiomas, spinal ependymomas, or further cranial nerve schwannomas. I would specify high-resolution, heavily T2-weighted 3D sequences (such as CISS or FIESTA) to perfectly delineate the cranial nerves, alongside pre- and post-contrast T1-weighted sequences to thoroughly screen the neuroaxis for associated meningiomas and intramedullary tumours. If managed conservatively or post-treatment, I would recommend lifelong surveillance MRI follow-up at regular intervals, guided by neuro-oncology protocols, due to the high risk of progressive or new syndromic tumours. This should go to the Neuro-oncology and Skull Base MDT. My report would clearly document the exact dimensions of both masses, the severity of brainstem and fourth ventricular compression, and the presence of any other intracranial or spinal lesions to guide complex multi-staged neurosurgical or radiosurgical management.
